topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was leaving. It was a sterile notification, devoid of the warmth that had characterized our weekly meetings for the past two years. I stared at the screen, the words blurring slightly. My hands began to tremble, a slight, involuntary shaking that I tried to suppress by gripping the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white. The silence of my small apartment amplified the sudden hollowness in my chest. I reread the message three times, searching for some hidden caveat, a softening phrase that would contradict the abrupt finality of the announcement. When I finally closed the laptop, the sound echoed in the quiet room, and I walked to the window, watching the familiar campus quad, feeling a profound, heavy stillness settle over me.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I managed to ask, my voice sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric of the seat digging into my spine. The air in the office felt suddenly thin, charged with an unspoken sense of finality. Dr. Albright was packing up his desk, the methodical click of his briefcase a sharp counterpoint to the churning in my stomach. I watched him, noting the way his shoulders seemed unusually rigid, a tension I hadn't noticed before. A wave of sudden, sharp anxiety washed over me, making my breath catch in my throat. I fumbled for my notebook, my fingers clumsy and uncoordinated, unable to focus on the complex arguments I had spent months building.

[story 3]

I kept pacing the length of my cramped dorm room, the floorboards protesting softly beneath my hurried steps. Every time I stopped, I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the dampness of my palms. The news—that my primary guide was departing—had stripped away the scaffolding I had built my entire academic year upon. A tightness constricted around my throat, making swallowing difficult. I slumped onto my bed, the mattress dipping under my weight, and stared blankly at the ceiling. The fluorescent light hummed relentlessly, and for a long moment, all I could register was the dull ache spreading through my limbs, a deep, pervasive sense of disorientation.
